mn117:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn117:10.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:10.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:10.3: One understands wrong intention as ‘wrong intention’, and understands right intention as ‘right intention’; this is one’s right view.
mn117:11.1: And what, bhikkhus, is wrong intention?
mn117:11.2: Intention of sensuality, intention of ill will, intention of harming—
mn117:12.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right intention?
mn117:12.2: Bhikkhus, I say that right intention is twofold:
mn117:12.3: there is right intention with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:12.4: and there is right intention that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:13.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right intention with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:13.2: Intention of renunciation, intention of non-ill will, intention of non-harming:
mn117:13.3: ‘this, bhikkhus, is right intention with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.’
mn117:14.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right intention that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor?
mn117:14.2: Whatever reflection, thought [*vitakko*], intention, placing, fixing, directing of the mind, verbal *saṅkhāra* [*vacīsaṅkhāro*], in one whose mind is noble [*ariyacittassa*], whose mind is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path, who is developing the noble path—
mn117:14.3: this, bhikkhus, is right intention that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:15.1: One strives for the abandoning of wrong intention, for the entering upon right intention; this is one’s right effort.
mn117:15.2: Mindful, one abandons wrong intention; mindful, one dwells having entered upon right intention; this is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:15.3: Thus these three dhammas run and circle around right intention, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:16.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:16.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:16.3: One understands wrong speech as ‘wrong speech’, and understands right speech as ‘right speech’; this is one’s right view.
mn117:17.1: And what, bhikkhus, is wrong speech?
mn117:17.2: False speech, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle chatter—
mn117:18.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right speech?
mn117:18.2: Bhikkhus, I say that right speech is twofold:
mn117:18.3: there is right speech with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:18.4: and there is right speech that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:19.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right speech with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:19.2: Abstaining from false speech, abstaining from divisive speech, abstaining from harsh speech, abstaining from idle chatter—
mn117:19.3: this, bhikkhus, is right speech with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:2.1: “Bhikkhus, I will teach you noble right *samādhi*, with its supporting conditions and its requisites.
mn117:2.2: Listen to that, attend to it closely. I will speak.”
mn117:20.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right speech that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], transcendent, a path factor?
mn117:20.2: The refraining, abstaining, holding back, and non-doing with regard to the four kinds of verbal misconduct, in one whose mind is noble [*ariyacittassa*], whose mind is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path, who is developing the noble path—
mn117:20.3: this, bhikkhus, is right speech that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:21.1: One strives for the abandoning of wrong speech, for the entering upon right speech; this is one’s right effort.
mn117:21.2: Mindful, one abandons wrong speech; mindful, one dwells having entered upon right speech; this is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:21.3: Thus these three dhammas run and circle around right speech, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:22.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:22.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:22.3: One understands wrong action as ‘wrong action’, and understands right action as ‘right action’; this is one’s right view.
mn117:23.1: And what, bhikkhus, is wrong action?
mn117:23.2: Killing living beings, taking what is not given, sexual misconduct—
mn117:24.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right action?
mn117:24.2: Bhikkhus, I say that right action is twofold:
mn117:24.3: there is right action with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:24.4: and there is right action that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:25.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right action with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:25.2: Abstaining from killing living beings, abstaining from taking what is not given, abstaining from sexual misconduct—
mn117:25.3: this, bhikkhus, is right action with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:26.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right action that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor?
mn117:26.2: The refraining, abstaining, holding back, and non-doing with regard to the three kinds of bodily misconduct, in one whose mind is noble [*ariyacittassa*], whose mind is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path, who is developing the noble path—
mn117:26.3: this, bhikkhus, is right action that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:27.1: One strives for the abandoning of wrong action, for the entering upon right action; this is one’s right effort.
mn117:27.2: Mindful, one abandons wrong action; mindful, one dwells having entered upon right action; this is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:27.3: Thus these three dhammas run and circle around right action, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:28.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:28.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:28.3: One understands wrong livelihood as ‘wrong livelihood’, and understands right livelihood as ‘right livelihood’; this is one’s right view.
mn117:29.1: And what, bhikkhus, is wrong livelihood?
mn117:29.2: Deceit, talk, hinting, belittling, pursuing gain with gain—
mn117:3.1: “And what, bhikkhus, is noble right *samādhi*, with its supporting conditions and its requisites?
mn117:3.2: That is: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*;
mn117:3.3: the singleness of mind [*citassa ekaggatā*] equipped with these seven factors—
mn117:3.4: this is called, bhikkhus, noble right *samādhi*, ‘with supporting conditions’ and also ‘with requisites’.
mn117:30.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right livelihood?
mn117:30.2: Bhikkhus, I say that right livelihood is twofold:
mn117:30.3: there is right livelihood with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:30.4: and there is right livelihood that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:31.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right livelihood with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:31.2: Here, bhikkhus, a noble disciple, having abandoned wrong livelihood, makes a living by right livelihood—
mn117:31.3: this, bhikkhus, is right livelihood with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:32.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right livelihood that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor?
mn117:32.2: The refraining, abstaining, holding back, and non-doing with regard to wrong livelihood, in one whose mind is noble [*ariyacittassa*], whose mind is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path, who is developing the noble path—
mn117:32.3: this, bhikkhus, is right livelihood that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:33.1: One strives for the abandoning of wrong livelihood, for the entering upon right livelihood; this is one’s right effort.
mn117:33.2: Mindful, one abandons wrong livelihood; mindful, one dwells having entered upon right livelihood; this is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:33.3: Thus these three dhammas run and circle around right livelihood, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:34.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:34.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:34.3: For one with right view, right intention arises; for one with right intention, right speech arises; for one with right speech, right action arises; for one with right action, right livelihood arises; for one with right livelihood, right effort arises; for one with right effort, right *sati* arises; for one with right *sati*, right *samādhi* arises; for one with right *samādhi*, right knowledge arises; for one with right knowledge, right liberation arises.
mn117:34.4: So it is, bhikkhus: a trainee is endowed with eight factors, and an arahant is endowed with ten factors.
mn117:34.5: There too, through right knowledge, many bad, unskillful dhammas are dispelled as development reaches fulfillment.
mn117:35.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:35.10: for one with right effort, bhikkhus, wrong effort is worn away …
mn117:35.11: for one with right *sati*, bhikkhus, wrong *sati* is worn away …
mn117:35.12: for one with right *samādhi*, bhikkhus, wrong *samādhi* is worn away …
mn117:35.13: for one with right knowledge, bhikkhus, wrong knowledge is worn away …
mn117:35.14: for one with right liberation, bhikkhus, wrong liberation is worn away.
mn117:35.15: And the many bad, unskillful dhammas that arise dependent on wrong liberation are also worn away for that one.
mn117:35.16: And dependent on right liberation, many skillful dhammas go to fulfillment in development.
mn117:35.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:35.3: For one with right view, wrong view is worn away.
mn117:35.4: And the many bad, unskillful dhammas that arise dependent on wrong view are also worn away for that one.
mn117:35.5: Dependent on right view, many skillful dhammas go to fulfillment in development.
mn117:35.6: For one with right intention, bhikkhus, wrong intention is worn away …
mn117:35.7: for one with right speech, bhikkhus, wrong speech is worn away …
mn117:35.8: for one with right action, bhikkhus, wrong action is worn away …
mn117:35.9: for one with right livelihood, bhikkhus, wrong livelihood is worn away …
mn117:36.1: So it is, bhikkhus: twenty skillful factors and twenty unskillful factors—
mn117:36.2: this exposition of dhamma on the Great Forty has been set rolling, and it cannot be turned back by any ascetic or brahmin or deva or Māra or Brahmā or anyone at all in the world.
mn117:37.1: For if any ascetic or brahmin should think that this exposition of dhamma on the Great Forty ought to be criticized and rejected, then in this very life ten dhamma-consistent grounds of rebuke and counter-rebuke, blameworthy grounds, come upon that person—
mn117:37.11: if you criticize right liberation, then those ascetics and brahmins who hold wrong liberation are the ones you honor, the ones you praise.
mn117:37.12: If any ascetic or brahmin should think that this exposition of dhamma on the Great Forty ought to be criticized and rejected, then these ten dhamma-consistent grounds of rebuke and counter-rebuke, blameworthy grounds, come upon that person in this very life.
mn117:37.2: if you criticize right view, then those ascetics and brahmins who hold wrong view are the ones you honor, the ones you praise;
mn117:37.3: if you criticize right intention, then those ascetics and brahmins who hold wrong intention are the ones you honor, the ones you praise;
mn117:38.1: Even those who were Okkalas and Vassabhaññas, proponents of non-causality, non-action, and nothingness, did not think that this exposition of dhamma on the Great Forty ought to be criticized or rejected.
mn117:38.3: For fear of blame, attack, and reproach.”
mn117:38.5: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn117:38.6: The Great Forty is finished, the seventh.
mn117:4.1: Therein, bhikkhus, right view comes first.
mn117:4.2: And how, bhikkhus, does right view come first?
mn117:4.3: One understands wrong view as ‘wrong view’, and understands right view as ‘right view’—this is one’s right view.
mn117:5.1: And what, bhikkhus, is wrong view?
mn117:5.2: ‘There is nothing given, nothing offered, nothing sacrificed; there is no fruit or result of good and bad kammas; there is no this world, no other world, no mother, no father, no beings who are spontaneously reborn; there are no ascetics and brahmins in the world who are faring rightly and practicing rightly, who, having directly known and realized for themselves this world and the other world, make them known’—
mn117:6.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right view?
mn117:6.2: Bhikkhus, I say that right view is twofold:
mn117:6.3: there is right view with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:6.4: and there is right view that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:7.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right view with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:7.2: ‘There is what is given, there is what is offered, there is what is sacrificed; there is fruit and result of good and bad kammas; there is this world, there is the other world, there is mother, there is father, there are beings who are spontaneously reborn; there are ascetics and brahmins in the world who are faring rightly and practicing rightly, who, having directly known and realized for themselves this world and the other world, make them known’—
mn117:7.3: this, bhikkhus, is right view with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:8.1: And what, bhikkhus, is right view that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], transcendent, a path factor?
mn117:8.2: The discernment [*paññā*], the discernment *indriya* [*paññindriyaṁ*], the discernment *bala* [*paññābalaṁ*], the investigation-of-dhammas *bojjhaṅga* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgo*], the right-view path factor, in one whose mind is noble [*ariyacittassa*], whose mind is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path, who is developing the noble path—
mn117:8.3: this is called, bhikkhus, right view that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], transcendent, a path factor.
mn117:9.1: One strives for the abandoning of wrong view, for the entering upon right view; this is one’s right effort.
mn117:9.2: Mindful, one abandons wrong view; mindful, one dwells having entered upon right view; this is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:9.3: Thus these three dhammas run and circle around right view, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
